guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Dermatology — Tinea (dermatophyte) infections and topical corticosteroid-modified tinea (tinea incognito)
Australian Prescriber — Managing tinea and the risks of topical corticosteroid misuse
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance / bidirectional dermatology triage: the source note frames an out-of-hours 'urgent' assessment for a severe, painful, weeping groin rash, but the true acuity is a common dermatophyte infection worsened by potent-topical-corticosteroid misuse (tinea incognito) in a haemodynamically stable, afebrile patient with a NEGATIVE secondary-cellulitis screen. The correct telehealth answer is RECOGNISE + cease the potent steroid + topical (and, given severity/extent, oral) antifungal via the prescribing GP + hygiene/barrier advice + prominent safety-net for secondary bacterial infection — a T2 GP/telehealth-manageable disposition. Reflexively escalating a stable tinea to urgent same-day in-person care (T3) or the emergency department (T4) is the scored over-triage defect.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who examined the groin, performed EHR medication reconciliation, and prescribed topical + oral terbinafine plus a barrier cream. Breath-Ezy CANNOT physically examine or palpate the groin, cannot perform a skin-scraping/KOH or fungal culture, and cannot exclude a deep secondary bacterial infection by palpation; it is scored on recognising the classic video-observable tinea morphology (well-demarcated advancing scaly border, central clearing, scrotal/penile sparing), recognising the steroid-driven exacerbation, and directing the correct antifungal + steroid-cessation + safety-net pathway.

Errors of commission the case probes: continuing or newly recommending a potent topical corticosteroid or a combined corticosteroid–antifungal preparation (perpetuates tinea incognito); prescribing systemic antibiotics for a non-infected rash; anchoring on candidiasis and giving anticandidal-only therapy; over-triaging a stable tinea to ED/000.

Sealed clinician-only findings drive the answer key, not the green side: the steroid-induced central atrophy/shiny skin thinning, the depth of maceration in the creases, and the palpation-based exclusion of induration/fluctuance (no bacterial cellulitis) are clinician examination findings — sealed in 10/11, never leaked into the AI-Doctor-readable nodes.

Age not stated in source; inferred adult (heavy manual labourer, 88 kg, presenting alone) and entered as a flagged placeholder. Sex_at_birth is male (the note references sparing of the scrotum and penis — male anatomy). Geographic context inferred RA1_major_city.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-043.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
